one_line_takeaway: With prompt engineering alone, GPT-4 flags ~92% of insecure Ghera Android apps and finds 6/8 Vuldroid bugs, but it over-flags secure apps badly (best case still ~58% false-positive on secure apps); adding short CWE-style vulnerability summaries is the biggest single win.
key_finding: GPT-4 detects insecure Android apps well but has a strong bias toward over-flagging: it catches most vulnerable apps yet also labels many secure apps as insecure. Adding a brief vulnerability summary (Exp 2) is the single most effective change, raising overall TP rate from 51.26% (Exp 1) to 66.38% and dramatically cutting false positives on secure apps (from 56/59 down to 34/59). The headline abstract figure of 91.67% refers to correctly flagging insecure apps in Experiment 2 (55 of 60 insecure apps flagged insecure). Coarse RAG (Exp 3) improves efficiency/scalability but slightly lowers TP rate to 60.5%, partly due to hallucinations induced by LLM-generated file summaries. Data-leakage matters: leaving the word 'secure' in inputs measurably skewed results, underscoring the need to sanitize semantic hints. On the Vuldroid case study, LLB correctly identified 6 of 8 seeded vulnerabilities (the 2 misses were 'undecidable' because the file-retrieval step failed to surface the relevant snippets, not because of wrong reasoning).
core_contribution: An exploratory study (a CS858 course project report from the University of Waterloo) on whether GPT-4, driven purely by prompt engineering with no fine-tuning, can detect vulnerabilities in Android applications. The authors run three progressively richer prompting experiments on the Ghera benchmark and package the resulting workflow into an open Python CLI tool called 'LLB' (llb) that scans an Android project directory and produces a report that both flags vulnerable code snippets and suggests fixes. The contribution is empirical/engineering rather than a new model or algorithm: it characterizes how basic prompting, adding short CWE-like vulnerability summaries, and coarse-grained file-request-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation each affect true-positive and false-positive rates, plus a case study on the intentionally-vulnerable Vuldroid app.
threat_model: The vulnerabilities targeted are standard Android app-level weaknesses catalogued by the Ghera benchmark: insecure inter-component communication (intent hijacking, intent sniffing, exported receivers/activities), WebView issues (XSS via exported activity or deep link, JavaScript-interface information exposure of GPS location without consent, local file:// access), crypto misuse, networking/MitM, weak permissions, insecure storage/SQL injection, and file-provider misconfiguration allowing file theft. The adversary is typically a co-located malicious app or a malicious webpage exploiting a benign but vulnerable app. Note: the paper positions the LLM as a developer-assistance detector, NOT as an adversary; there is no adversarial-ML threat model against the LLM itself (prompt injection / data poisoning are not studied). The only LLM-side integrity concern raised is inadvertent ground-truth leakage from benchmark folder/file names.
limitations: Explicitly a course-project 'project proposal' report, not a full peer-reviewed empirical study. Single model evaluated end-to-end (GPT-4); results acknowledged to vary over time despite fixed seeds. No empirical baseline against static/dynamic analysis or prior ML detectors is run, so 'outperforms' claims are relative to the authors' expectations, not measured tools. Persistently high false-positive rate on secure apps (best case still 34/59 secure apps flagged insecure in Exp 2, 43/59 in Exp 3). Ghera consists of small 'lean' single-vulnerability apps, so real-world scalability to large codebases is asserted but not demonstrated; the RAG mechanism is coarse (file-level, builds outward from a fixed file set) and the paper itself proposes vector-DB indexing as the fix. Summaries induce hallucinations and were therefore excluded from the shipped LLB package. Residual, unaccounted-for ground-truth leakage (e.g., folder names hinting at vulnerability class) is admitted. Effectiveness is heavily dependent on prompt-engineering skill, and no critique/self-verification loop is yet implemented to curb over-flagging.

datasets_used: Ghera Android security benchmark (69 catalogued vulnerability types across Crypto, ICC, Networking, NonAPI, Permission, Storage, System, Web; each with benign-vulnerable, malicious, and secure app variants; ~60 insecure and 59 secure apps used in the experiments)
Vuldroid (intentionally-vulnerable Android app with 8 seeded vulnerabilities V1-V8, used as an end-to-end case study)
quantitative_results: Experiment 1 (basic prompting, cleaned data): 58/60 insecure apps flagged insecure, but 56/59 secure apps misclassified as insecure; TP rate 51.26%

Experiment 2 (with short vulnerability summary, cleaned data): 55/60 insecure flagged insecure (= 91.67%, the abstract's headline number), 3 flagged secure, 2 undecidable; secure apps 34/59 flagged insecure vs 24 flagged secure; TP rate 66.38%
Experiment 3 (on-demand file-request RAG, cleaned data): 57/60 insecure flagged insecure, 2 flagged secure, 1 undecidable; secure apps 43/59 flagged insecure, 15 flagged secure; TP rate 60.5%
Experiment 1 leaked-data run: secure apps 38/59 correctly flagged secure (vs only 3/59 in cleaned run), showing the word 'secure' in inputs strongly skewed outcomes
Vuldroid case study: 6 of 8 seeded vulnerabilities correctly detected (V2,V3,V4,V6,V7,V8 = detected; V1,V5 = undecidable due to file-retrieval failure)
baselines_compared: No baseline detector is empirically run in this paper; comparison to static/dynamic analysis and prior ML approaches is discussed qualitatively and deferred to future work

Internal model comparison only: GPT-4 selected over GPT-3.5 (failed to follow instructions) and Llama 2 (performed significantly worse); GPT-4 also compared against itself across the three prompting configurations
Prior GPT-based detection results (e.g., Cheshkov et al. 2023) are cited as related work for context, not reproduced as baselines
